tldr: Headlines from all 16 outlet buckets converge on a single register: the 50% tariff rate and commodity specifics (wine, hockey sticks, dairy, cement), with almost no factual variation. A secondary fairness strand — citing US claims of Canadian 'discrimination' — surfaces explicitly in titles from Qatar, the US, UK, Canada, and India outlets; a handful of headlines embed bilateral-relations or trade-war framing (BBC uniquely captures Carney's diplomatic response; El Pais names the USMCA context), while only Politico and CBC frame the already-signed orders as a political 'threat' rather than a fait accompli.

single_outlet_findings.finding: The only outlet to headline the signed executive orders as a 'threat' rather than an enacted tariff, framing a completed action as a political pressure play — the sharpest headline-level departure from the factual baseline in the corpus.

The only headline to include Canada's diplomatic counter-response (Carney vowing to 'intensify' trade talks) as a headline-level fact rather than a body detail, making the bilateral dimension visible before the click.
The only headline to name the USMCA negotiating context explicitly, framing the tariff action as part of an ongoing treaty renegotiation rather than a standalone punitive measure.

The only single-word headline in the corpus ('Tariffs'), stripping all framing, context, and attribution — the body alleges the tariffs are illegal under defunct Smoot-Hawley law, but no trace of that angle reaches the headline layer.

bottom_line: At the headline layer, the Canada tariff story is almost uniformly framed as an economic action — the 50% rate and commodity list dominate across all 16 buckets — while the legal controversy over Section 338, the diplomatic response, and the CUSMA leverage logic that animate body text remain almost entirely below the fold.
